name: prometheus-client

metadata.name: prometheus-client
classifiers: License :: OSI Approved :: Apache Software License
metadata.summary: Python client for the Prometheus monitoring system.

documentation:
# Prometheus Python Client

The official Python client for [Prometheus](https://prometheus.io).

## Installation

```
pip install prometheus-client
```

This package can be found on [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/prometheus_client).

## Documentation

Documentation is available on https://prometheus.github.io/client_python

## Links

* [Releases](https://github.com/prometheus/client_python/releases): The releases page shows the history of the project and acts as a changelog.
* [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/prometheus_client)

methods.name: prometheus_client.multiprocess.MultiProcessCollector.merge
documentation:
Merge metrics from given mmap files.

By default, histograms are accumulated, as per prometheus wire format.
But if writing the merged data back to mmap files, use
accumulate=False to avoid compound accumulation.
Collector for files for multi-process mode.

functions.name: prometheus_client.mmap_dict._pack_two_doubles
documentation: struct.pack_into has atomicity issues because it will temporarily write 0 into
the mmap, resulting in false reads to 0 when experiencing a lot of writes.
Using direct assignment solves this issue.

functions.name: prometheus_client.mmap_dict._read_all_values
documentation: Yield (key, value, timestamp, pos). No locking is performed.

functions.name: prometheus_client.mmap_dict.mmap_key
documentation: Format a key for use in the mmap file.

classes.name: prometheus_client.mmap_dict.MmapedDict

methods.name: prometheus_client.mmap_dict.MmapedDict._init_value
documentation: Initialize a value. Lock must be held by caller.

methods.name: prometheus_client.mmap_dict.MmapedDict._read_all_values
documentation: Yield (key, value, pos). No locking is performed.

methods.name: prometheus_client.mmap_dict.MmapedDict.read_all_values
documentation: Yield (key, value, timestamp). No locking is performed.

A dict of doubles, backed by an mmapped file.

The file starts with a 4 byte int, indicating how much of it is used.
Then 4 bytes of padding.
There's then a number of entries, consisting of a 4 byte int which is the
size of the next field, a utf-8 encoded string key, padding to a 8 byte
alignment, and then a 8 byte float which is the value and a 8 byte float
which is a UNIX timestamp in seconds.

Not thread safe.

functions.name: prometheus_client.parser.text_string_to_metric_families
documentation:
Parse Prometheus text format from a unicode string.

See text_fd_to_metric_families.

functions.name: prometheus_client.parser._parse_value_and_timestamp
documentation: If we have multiple values only consider the first

functions.name: prometheus_client.parser.text_fd_to_metric_families
documentation:
Parse Prometheus text format from a file descriptor.

This is a laxer parser than the main Go parser,
so successful parsing does not imply that the parsed
text meets the specification.

Yields Metric's.

classes.name: prometheus_client.samples.Exemplar
documentation: Timestamp and exemplar are optional.
Value can be an int or a float.
Timestamp can be a float containing a unixtime in seconds,
a Timestamp object, or None.
Exemplar can be an Exemplar object, or None.

functions.name: prometheus_client.values.MultiProcessValue
documentation:
Returns a MmapedValue class based on a process_identifier function.

The 'process_identifier' function MUST comply with this simple rule:
when called in simultaneously running processes it MUST return distinct values.

Using a different function than the default 'os.getpid' is at your own risk.

classes.name: prometheus_client.values.MutexValue
documentation: A float protected by a mutex.

methods.name: prometheus_client.registry.CollectorRegistry.restricted_registry
documentation:
Returns object that only collects some metrics.

Returns an object which upon collect() will return
only samples with the given names.

Intended usage is:
    generate_latest(REGISTRY.restricted_registry(['a_timeseries']))

Experimental.

methods.name: prometheus_client.registry.CollectorRegistry.get_sample_value
documentation:
Returns the sample value, or None if not found.

This is inefficient, and intended only for use in unittests.

Metric collector registry.

Collectors must have a no-argument method 'collect' that returns a list of
Metric objects. The returned metrics should be consistent with the Prometheus
exposition formats.

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.Metric

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.Metric.add_sample
documentation:
Add a sample to the metric.

Internal-only, do not use.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.Metric._restricted_metric
documentation: Build a snapshot of a metric with samples restricted to a given set of names.

A single metric family and its samples.

This is intended only for internal use by the instrumentation client.

Custom collectors should use GaugeMetricFamily, CounterMetricFamily
and SummaryMetricFamily instead.

documentation:
Add a metric to the metric family.
Args:
labels: A list of label values
value: The value of the metric.

A single unknown metric and its samples.
For use by custom collectors.

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.CounterMetricFamily

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.CounterMetricFamily.add_metric

Add a metric to the metric family.

Args:

  created: Optional unix timestamp the child was created at.

A single counter and its samples.

For use by custom collectors.

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.GaugeMetricFamily

Add a metric to the metric family.

Add a metric to the metric family.

Args:

A single summary and its samples.

For use by custom collectors.

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.HistogramMetricFamily

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.HistogramMetricFamily.add_metric
documentation:
Add a metric to the metric family.

Args:
  labels: A list of label values
  buckets: A list of lists.
      Each inner list can be a pair of bucket name and value,
      or a triple of bucket name, value, and exemplar.
      The buckets must be sorted, and +Inf present.
  sum_value: The sum value of the metric.

A single histogram and its samples.

For use by custom collectors.

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.GaugeHistogramMetricFamily

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.GaugeHistogramMetricFamily.add_metric
documentation:
Add a metric to the metric family.

Args:
  labels: A list of label values
  buckets: A list of pairs of bucket names and values.
      The buckets must be sorted, and +Inf present.
  gsum_value: The sum value of the metric.

A single gauge histogram and its samples.

For use by custom collectors.

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics_core.InfoMetricFamily

Add a metric to the metric family.

Args:

A single info and its samples.

For use by custom collectors.

For use by custom collectors.

Create a function from the strings name, signature and body.
evaldict is the evaluation dictionary. If addsource is true an
attribute __source__ is added to the result. The attributes attrs
are added, if any.

An object with the ability to create functions with a given signature.
It has attributes name, doc, module, signature, defaults, dict and
methods update and make.
basic functionality

documentation: Create a WSGI app which serves the metrics from a registry.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition._get_best_family
documentation: Automatically select address family depending on address

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition._get_ssl_ctx
documentation: Load context supports SSL.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.start_wsgi_server
documentation: Starts a WSGI server for prometheus metrics as a daemon thread.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.generate_latest
documentation: Returns the metrics from the registry in latest text format as a string.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.write_to_textfile
documentation:
Write metrics to the given path.

This is intended for use with the Node exporter textfile collector.
The path must end in .prom for the textfile collector to process it.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.default_handler
documentation:
Default handler that implements HTTP/HTTPS connections.

Used by the push_to_gateway functions. Can be re-used by other handlers.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.passthrough_redirect_handler
documentation:
Handler that automatically trusts redirect responses for all HTTP methods.

Augments standard HTTPRedirectHandler capability by permitting PUT requests,
preserving the method upon redirect, and passing through all headers and
data from the original request. Only use this handler if you control or
trust the source of redirect responses you encounter when making requests
via the Prometheus client. This handler will simply repeat the identical
request, including same method and data, to the new redirect URL.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.basic_auth_handler
documentation:
Handler that implements HTTP/HTTPS connections with Basic Auth.

Sets auth headers using supplied 'username' and 'password', if set.
Used by the push_to_gateway functions. Can be re-used by other handlers.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.tls_auth_handler
documentation:
Handler that implements an HTTPS connection with TLS Auth.

The default protocol (ssl.PROTOCOL_TLS_CLIENT) will also enable
ssl.CERT_REQUIRED and SSLContext.check_hostname by default. This can be
disabled by setting insecure_skip_verify to True.

Both this handler and the TLS feature on pushgateay are experimental.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.push_to_gateway
documentation:
Push metrics to the given pushgateway.

`gateway` the url for your push gateway. Either of the form
          'http://pushgateway.local', or 'pushgateway.local'.
          Scheme defaults to 'http' if none is provided
`job` is the job label to be attached to all pushed metrics
`registry` is an instance of CollectorRegistry
`grouping_key` please see the pushgateway documentation for details.
               Defaults to None
`timeout` is how long push will attempt to connect before giving up.
          Defaults to 30s, can be set to None for no timeout.
`handler` is an optional function which can be provided to perform
          requests to the 'gateway'.
          Defaults to None, in which case an http or https request
          will be carried out by a default handler.
          If not None, the argument must be a function which accepts
          the following arguments:
          url, method, timeout, headers, and content
          May be used to implement additional functionality not
          supported by the built-in default handler (such as SSL
          client certicates, and HTTP authentication mechanisms).
          'url' is the URL for the request, the 'gateway' argument
          described earlier will form the basis of this URL.
          'method' is the HTTP method which should be used when
          carrying out the request.
          'timeout' requests not successfully completed after this
          many seconds should be aborted.  If timeout is None, then
          the handler should not set a timeout.
          'headers' is a list of ("header-name","header-value") tuples
          which must be passed to the pushgateway in the form of HTTP
          request headers.
          The function should raise an exception (e.g. IOError) on
          failure.
          'content' is the data which should be used to form the HTTP
          Message Body.

This overwrites all metrics with the same job and grouping_key.
This uses the PUT HTTP method.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.pushadd_to_gateway
documentation:
PushAdd metrics to the given pushgateway.

`gateway` the url for your push gateway. Either of the form
          'http://pushgateway.local', or 'pushgateway.local'.
          Scheme defaults to 'http' if none is provided
`job` is the job label to be attached to all pushed metrics
`registry` is an instance of CollectorRegistry
`grouping_key` please see the pushgateway documentation for details.
               Defaults to None
`timeout` is how long push will attempt to connect before giving up.
          Defaults to 30s, can be set to None for no timeout.
`handler` is an optional function which can be provided to perform
          requests to the 'gateway'.
          Defaults to None, in which case an http or https request
          will be carried out by a default handler.
          See the 'prometheus_client.push_to_gateway' documentation
          for implementation requirements.

This replaces metrics with the same name, job and grouping_key.
This uses the POST HTTP method.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.delete_from_gateway
documentation:
Delete metrics from the given pushgateway.

`gateway` the url for your push gateway. Either of the form
          'http://pushgateway.local', or 'pushgateway.local'.
          Scheme defaults to 'http' if none is provided
`job` is the job label to be attached to all pushed metrics
`grouping_key` please see the pushgateway documentation for details.
               Defaults to None
`timeout` is how long delete will attempt to connect before giving up.
          Defaults to 30s, can be set to None for no timeout.
`handler` is an optional function which can be provided to perform
          requests to the 'gateway'.
          Defaults to None, in which case an http or https request
          will be carried out by a default handler.
          See the 'prometheus_client.push_to_gateway' documentation
          for implementation requirements.

This deletes metrics with the given job and grouping_key.
This uses the DELETE HTTP method.

functions.name: prometheus_client.exposition.instance_ip_grouping_key
documentation: Grouping key with instance set to the IP Address of this host.

variables.name: prometheus_client.exposition.CONTENT_TYPE_LATEST
documentation: Content type of the latest text format

classes.name: prometheus_client.exposition._PrometheusRedirectHandler

methods.name: prometheus_client.exposition._PrometheusRedirectHandler.redirect_request
documentation:
Apply redirect logic to a request.

See parent HTTPRedirectHandler.redirect_request for parameter info.

If the redirect is disallowed, this raises the corresponding HTTP error.
If the redirect can't be determined, return None to allow other handlers
to try. If the redirect is allowed, return the new request.

This method specialized for the case when (a) the user knows that the
redirect will not cause unacceptable side effects for any request method,
and (b) the user knows that any request data should be passed through to
the redirect. If either condition is not met, this should not be used.

Allow additional methods (e.g. PUT) and data forwarding in redirects.

Use of this class constitute a user's explicit agreement to the
redirect responses the Prometheus client will receive when using it.
You should only use this class if you control or otherwise trust the
redirect behavior involved and are certain it is safe to full transfer
the original request (method and data) to the redirected URL. For
example, if you know there is a cosmetic URL redirect in front of a
local deployment of a Prometheus server, and all redirects are safe,
this is the class to use to handle redirects in that case.

The standard HTTPRedirectHandler does not forward request data nor
does it allow redirected PUT requests (which Prometheus uses for some
operations, for example `push_to_gateway`) because these cannot
generically guarantee no violations of HTTP RFC 2616 requirements for
the user to explicitly confirm redirects that could have unexpected
side effects (such as rendering a PUT request non-idempotent or
creating multiple resources not named in the original request).

classes.name: prometheus_client.exposition._SilentHandler

methods.name: prometheus_client.exposition._SilentHandler.log_message
documentation: Log nothing.
WSGI handler that does not log requests.

classes.name: prometheus_client.exposition.ThreadingWSGIServer
documentation: Thread per request HTTP server.

classes.name: prometheus_client.exposition.MetricsHandler

methods.name: prometheus_client.exposition.MetricsHandler.log_message
documentation: Log nothing.

methods.name: prometheus_client.exposition.MetricsHandler.factory
documentation:
Returns a dynamic MetricsHandler class tied
to the passed registry.
HTTP handler that gives metrics from `REGISTRY`.

functions.name: prometheus_client.metrics.disable_created_metrics
documentation: Disable exporting _created metrics on counters, histograms, and summaries.

functions.name: prometheus_client.metrics.enable_created_metrics
documentation: Enable exporting _created metrics on counters, histograms, and summaries.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.MetricWrapperBase.labels
documentation:
Return the child for the given labelset.

All metrics can have labels, allowing grouping of related time series.
Taking a counter as an example:

    from prometheus_client import Counter

    c = Counter('my_requests_total', 'HTTP Failures', ['method', 'endpoint'])
    c.labels('get', '/').inc()
    c.labels('post', '/submit').inc()

Labels can also be provided as keyword arguments:

    from prometheus_client import Counter

    c = Counter('my_requests_total', 'HTTP Failures', ['method', 'endpoint'])
    c.labels(method='get', endpoint='/').inc()
    c.labels(method='post', endpoint='/submit').inc()

See the best practices on [naming](http://prometheus.io/docs/practices/naming/)
and [labels](http://prometheus.io/docs/practices/instrumentation/#use-labels).

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.MetricWrapperBase.clear
documentation: Remove all labelsets from the metric

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.MetricWrapperBase._metric_init
documentation:
Initialize the metric object as a child, i.e. when it has labels (if any) set.

This is factored as a separate function to allow for deferred initialization.

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Counter

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Counter.inc
documentation: Increment counter by the given amount.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Counter.reset
documentation: Reset the counter to zero. Use this when a logical process restarts without restarting the actual python process.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Counter.count_exceptions
documentation:
Count exceptions in a block of code or function.

Can be used as a function decorator or context manager.
Increments the counter when an exception of the given
type is raised up out of the code.

A Counter tracks counts of events or running totals.

Example use cases for Counters:
- Number of requests processed
- Number of items that were inserted into a queue
- Total amount of data that a system has processed

Counters can only go up (and be reset when the process restarts). If your use case can go down,
you should use a Gauge instead.

An example for a Counter:

    from prometheus_client import Counter

    c = Counter('my_failures_total', 'Description of counter')
    c.inc()     # Increment by 1
    c.inc(1.6)  # Increment by given value

There are utilities to count exceptions raised:

    @c.count_exceptions()
    def f():
        pass

    with c.count_exceptions():
        pass

    # Count only one type of exception
    with c.count_exceptions(ValueError):
        pass
        
You can also reset the counter to zero in case your logical "process" restarts
without restarting the actual python process.

   c.reset()

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Gauge

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Gauge.inc
documentation: Increment gauge by the given amount.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Gauge.dec
documentation: Decrement gauge by the given amount.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Gauge.set
documentation: Set gauge to the given value.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Gauge.set_to_current_time
documentation: Set gauge to the current unixtime.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Gauge.track_inprogress
documentation:
Track inprogress blocks of code or functions.

Can be used as a function decorator or context manager.
Increments the gauge when the code is entered,
and decrements when it is exited.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Gauge.time
documentation:
Time a block of code or function, and set the duration in seconds.

Can be used as a function decorator or context manager.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Gauge.set_function
documentation:
Call the provided function to return the Gauge value.

The function must return a float, and may be called from
multiple threads. All other methods of the Gauge become NOOPs.

Gauge metric, to report instantaneous values.

Examples of Gauges include:
   - Inprogress requests
   - Number of items in a queue
   - Free memory
   - Total memory
   - Temperature

Gauges can go both up and down.

   from prometheus_client import Gauge

   g = Gauge('my_inprogress_requests', 'Description of gauge')
   g.inc()      # Increment by 1
   g.dec(10)    # Decrement by given value
   g.set(4.2)   # Set to a given value

There are utilities for common use cases:

   g.set_to_current_time()   # Set to current unixtime

   # Increment when entered, decrement when exited.
   @g.track_inprogress()
   def f():
       pass

   with g.track_inprogress():
       pass

A Gauge can also take its value from a callback:

   d = Gauge('data_objects', 'Number of objects')
   my_dict = {}
   d.set_function(lambda: len(my_dict))

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Summary

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Summary.observe
documentation:
Observe the given amount.

The amount is usually positive or zero. Negative values are
accepted but prevent current versions of Prometheus from
properly detecting counter resets in the sum of
observations. See
https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/histograms/#count-and-sum-of-observations
for details.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Summary.time
documentation:
Time a block of code or function, and observe the duration in seconds.

Can be used as a function decorator or context manager.

A Summary tracks the size and number of events.

Example use cases for Summaries:
- Response latency
- Request size

Example for a Summary:

    from prometheus_client import Summary

    s = Summary('request_size_bytes', 'Request size (bytes)')
    s.observe(512)  # Observe 512 (bytes)

Example for a Summary using time:

    from prometheus_client import Summary

    REQUEST_TIME = Summary('response_latency_seconds', 'Response latency (seconds)')

    @REQUEST_TIME.time()
    def create_response(request):
      '''A dummy function'''
      time.sleep(1)

Example for using the same Summary object as a context manager:

    with REQUEST_TIME.time():
        pass  # Logic to be timed

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Histogram

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Histogram.observe
documentation:
Observe the given amount.

The amount is usually positive or zero. Negative values are
accepted but prevent current versions of Prometheus from
properly detecting counter resets in the sum of
observations. See
https://prometheus.io/docs/practices/histograms/#count-and-sum-of-observations
for details.

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Histogram.time
documentation:
Time a block of code or function, and observe the duration in seconds.

Can be used as a function decorator or context manager.

A Histogram tracks the size and number of events in buckets.

You can use Histograms for aggregatable calculation of quantiles.

Example use cases:
- Response latency
- Request size

Example for a Histogram:

    from prometheus_client import Histogram

    h = Histogram('request_size_bytes', 'Request size (bytes)')
    h.observe(512)  # Observe 512 (bytes)

Example for a Histogram using time:

    from prometheus_client import Histogram

    REQUEST_TIME = Histogram('response_latency_seconds', 'Response latency (seconds)')

    @REQUEST_TIME.time()
    def create_response(request):
      '''A dummy function'''
      time.sleep(1)

Example of using the same Histogram object as a context manager:

    with REQUEST_TIME.time():
        pass  # Logic to be timed

The default buckets are intended to cover a typical web/rpc request from milliseconds to seconds.
They can be overridden by passing `buckets` keyword argument to `Histogram`.

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Info

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Info.info
documentation: Set info metric.

Info metric, key-value pairs.

Examples of Info include:
   - Build information
   - Version information
   - Potential target metadata

Example usage:
   from prometheus_client import Info

   i = Info('my_build', 'Description of info')
   i.info({'version': '1.2.3', 'buildhost': 'foo@bar'})

Info metrics do not work in multiprocess mode.

classes.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Enum

methods.name: prometheus_client.metrics.Enum.state
documentation: Set enum metric state.

Enum metric, which of a set of states is true.

Example usage:
   from prometheus_client import Enum

   e = Enum('task_state', 'Description of enum',
     states=['starting', 'running', 'stopped'])
   e.state('running')

The first listed state will be the default.
Enum metrics do not work in multiprocess mode.

modules.name: prometheus_client.openmetrics.parser
documentation: !/usr/bin/env python

functions.name: prometheus_client.openmetrics.parser.text_string_to_metric_families
documentation:
Parse Openmetrics text format from a unicode string.

See text_fd_to_metric_families.

functions.name: prometheus_client.openmetrics.parser.text_fd_to_metric_families
documentation:
Parse Prometheus text format from a file descriptor.

This is a laxer parser than the main Go parser,
so successful parsing does not imply that the parsed
text meets the specification.

Yields Metric's.
